← Docs index

MockingHAL — Configuration Guide

A MockingHAL configuration is a YAML (or JSON) document whose top-level keys are arbitrary labels. Each label maps to a TreeNode — a nested structure of request-matching filters and response elements. Requests are matched against all loaded nodes in insertion order; the first match wins.

Pages

PageCovers
Structure Document format, top-level labels, TreeNode anatomy, loading API
Routing filters method and path — strict filters that route or reject
Selectors query, headerIn, body — optional response selectors
Response elements code, resource, headerOut — how responses are built
Combining filters AND semantics, child chaining, multiple filter types at one node
YAML tips Anchors, quoting regex chars, multiline strings
Examples Complete worked configurations you can load and try
Load configuration Interactive form to submit or append a configuration to this server

Quick reference — TreeNode fields

FieldTypeKindPurpose
methodmap: method→nodestrict filterRoute by HTTP method; no match → 404
pathmap: regex→nodestrict filterRoute by path prefix; strips match; no match → 404
querymap: regex→nodeselectorOverride response when query matches; fall-through on miss
headerInmap: name→{regex→node}selectorOverride response when request header matches; fall-through on miss
bodymap: regex→nodeselectorOverride response when body matches; fall-through on miss
codeintegerresponseHTTP status code (default 200)
resourceanyresponseResponse body; strings sent verbatim, objects serialised as JSON
headerOutmap: name→valueresponseResponse headers; child values override parent values
All filter types present at a node are AND-combined. Strict filters (method, path) return 404 when unmatched. Selectors (query, headerIn, body) fall through to the node's own response elements when unmatched.

/structure.resource:
Docs › Config guide › Structure

Configuration Structure

Document format

A configuration document is a YAML or JSON object. MockingHAL accepts both formats everywhere — at startup from classpath files and at runtime via the loading API.

Top-level keys are arbitrary labels used only for identification and ordering. Each label maps to a TreeNode. 

label_one:
  # ... TreeNode fields ...

label_two:
  # ... TreeNode fields ...

Requests are matched against all loaded top-level nodes in insertion order. The first node whose tree produces a match wins. Put more specific rules before more general catch-alls.

TreeNode fields

Every node may contain any mix of filter fields and response fields. All are optional — an empty node {} is a valid leaf that returns the response accumulated so far.

Filter fields

FieldValue typeBehaviour
method map: METHOD → TreeNode Dispatches on the HTTP method. Keys are upper-case strings (GET, POST, …). Strict: no matching key → no match.
path map: regex → TreeNode Matches a prefix of the remaining path with an anchored regex. The matched prefix is stripped before recursing. Strict: no pattern matches → no match.
query map: regex → TreeNode Containment regex against the raw query string. Optional selector: no match falls through to the node's own response.
headerIn map: header-name → { regex → TreeNode } Containment regex against a request header value. Optional selector.
body map: regex → TreeNode Containment regex against the request body. Optional selector.

Response fields

FieldValue typeBehaviour
code integer HTTP status code. Default is 200. A child's value overrides a parent's.
resource any YAML value Response body. Strings are sent verbatim; maps and lists are serialised as JSON. A child's value overrides a parent's.
headerOut map: name → value Response headers. Maps from all nodes along the matched path are merged; child values override parent values for the same header name.

Loading API

MethodPathEffect
POST/ Replaces all loaded resources with the body. Previous config is discarded.
PATCH/ Merges the body into the current config. Top-level keys that collide are replaced; all others are preserved.

Both endpoints accept application/yaml, application/json, multipart/form-data, and multipart/mixed. For multipart, each part is parsed as a separate config block and merged in document order. 

# Replace everything
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/ \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/yaml' \
  --data-binary @my-api.yaml

# Add routes without losing the rest
curl -X PATCH http://localhost:8080/ \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/yaml' \
  --data-binary @extra-routes.yaml

Classpath defaults

On startup, MockingHAL loads every file found in the default/ classpath directory in alphabetical order. Files loaded earlier take precedence for matching because roots are tried in insertion order. Place more specific configs in files that sort earlier (e.g. aaa-specific.yaml before zzz-catchall.yaml).

/routing.resource:
Docs › Config guide › Routing filters

Routing Filters: method and path

Strict filters define the structure of your API. When a request fails to match a strict filter the entire branch is rejected — the matcher moves on to the next top-level entry. No fallback, no partial match.

method

Maps HTTP method names (upper-case) to child nodes. The request method must exactly match one of the keys. 

my_endpoint:
  method:
    GET:
      code: 200
      resource: {message: "fetched"}
    POST:
      code: 201
      resource: {message: "created"}
    DELETE:
      code: 204
At most one method filter should appear along any root-to-leaf path. A second method filter nested inside a child would only match if both method keys are the same string — which is always redundant.

path

Maps regex patterns to child nodes. Patterns are tested in document order. The first pattern that matches as a prefix of the remaining path wins.

Prefix matching rules

  • The regex is anchored at position 0 of the remaining path.
  • After the match, the remaining path must be empty or start with / (segment boundary). This prevents /foo from matching /foobar.
  • The matched prefix is stripped from the remaining path before the child node is evaluated. Nested path filters see only the unconsumed suffix.
  • An empty-string key "" matches only when the remaining path is itself empty — i.e. the path has been fully consumed.
api:
  path:
    /api:
      path:
        /users:
          method:
            GET:
              code: 200
              resource: [{id: 1, name: Alice}]
        /products:
          method:
            GET:
              code: 200
              resource: [{id: 42, name: Widget}]

A GET /api/users request strips /api, then /users, leaving an empty path, and returns the users list. A GET /api/orders request fails the inner path filter and returns 404.

Regex in path patterns

Because patterns are full regexes you can match variable segments: 

user_by_id:
  path:
    "/users/[0-9]+":
      method:
        GET:
          code: 200
          resource: {id: 1, name: "Example user"}
Remember to escape regex metacharacters that should be literal. A dot . in a URL segment matches any character unless written as \. (in YAML: "\\.").

Optional trailing slash

Use the pattern "/?"" (zero or one slash) to match a path both with and without a trailing slash, without repeating the child node: 

docs_root:
  path:
    /docs:
      method:
        GET:
          code: 200
          path:
            "/?":           # matches /docs and /docs/
              resource: "Welcome"
            /intro:         # matches /docs/intro
              resource: "Introduction"

For /docs/intro the pattern /? would match only the leading /, leaving intro — which fails the segment boundary check, so the matcher tries the next pattern /intro, which succeeds.

/selectors.resource:
Docs › Config guide › Selectors

Selectors: query, headerIn, body

Selectors are optional response overrides. When a selector's pattern matches, its child node is added to the matching chain and can override the current node's response elements. When no pattern matches the selector is silently skipped and the node's own response elements are used as-is.

This makes selectors ideal for content negotiation and conditional variants — the default response lives on the node itself, and selectors layer specific overrides on top.

query

Maps regex patterns to child nodes. The pattern is tested as a containment match against the full raw query string (everything after ?, without the leading ?). Patterns are tried in document order; the first match wins. 

search:
  path:
    /search:
      method:
        GET:
          code: 200
          resource: {results: []}          # default: empty results
          query:
            "q=hello":
              resource: {results: ["Hello world"]}
            "q=bye":
              resource: {results: ["Goodbye"]}

GET /search?q=hello returns the first override. GET /search?q=other returns the default empty results.

An empty-string key "" matches only when the query string is completely absent. Use it to distinguish "no query parameters at all" from any other case.

headerIn

Maps a single header name to a sub-map of regex patterns → child nodes. The pattern is tested as a containment match against the header's value. A missing header is treated as an empty string. 

content_negotiation:
  path:
    /data:
      method:
        GET:
          code: 200
          headerOut:
            Content-Type: application/json
          resource: {"format": "json"}     # default
          headerIn:
            Accept:
              "text/csv":
                headerOut:
                  Content-Type: text/csv
                resource: "format,value\ncsv,true\n"
              "text/plain":
                headerOut:
                  Content-Type: text/plain
                resource: "plain text response"

A request with Accept: text/csv gets the CSV response. A request with Accept: application/json or no Accept header gets the default JSON response.

Only the first entry in a headerIn map is used. To require two headers simultaneously, nest a second headerIn inside the child node of the first.

Matching multiple header values

List patterns inside the header's sub-map — they are tried in order and the first match wins. Use ".*" as the last pattern for an explicit catch-all that also adds a child node (as opposed to the implicit fall-through): 

headerIn:
  Accept:
    "application/hal\\+xml":
      resource: ""
    "application/hal\\+yaml":
      resource: "_links: {}"
    ".*":                          # explicit catch-all
      resource: {"_links": {}}

body

Maps regex patterns to child nodes. The pattern is tested as a containment match against the raw request body string. 

echo_variant:
  path:
    /submit:
      method:
        POST:
          code: 200
          resource: {status: "received"}    # default
          body:
            "urgent":
              code: 202
              resource: {status: "queued for priority processing"}
            "delete":
              code: 400
              resource: {error: "delete not allowed here"}

A POST /submit with body {"type":"urgent","data":...} matches the first pattern and returns 202. A body containing neither urgent nor delete returns the default 200.

Selector fall-through summary

SituationResult
Pattern matches Child node is chained; its response elements override the current node's
No pattern matches Selector is skipped; current node's own code, resource, headerOut are used
No resource on current node or ancestors Response body is empty; status code is the accumulated code (default 200)

/response.resource:
Docs › Config guide › Response elements

Response Elements

Response elements (code, resource, headerOut) are accumulated as the matcher traverses the tree from root to leaf. Each node contributes its own values; child values override or extend parent values.

code

An integer HTTP status code. The default is 200. A child's code overrides any ancestor's. 

example:
  code: 200          # default for the whole subtree
  method:
    GET:  {}          # inherits 200
    POST:
      code: 201      # overrides to 201
    DELETE:
      code: 204      # overrides to 204

resource

The response body. Accepts any YAML value:

YAML typeSent asExample
String (any style) Verbatim, byte-for-byte HTML, XML, plain text, pre-formatted JSON
Map / object Serialised to JSON resource: {id: 1, name: Alice}
List / array Serialised to JSON resource: [{id: 1}, {id: 2}]
Number / boolean Serialised to JSON resource: 42 → body 42

Use a YAML literal block (|) to send multi-line strings (HTML, XML) without escaping: 

page:
  resource: |
    <!DOCTYPE html>
    <html><body>Hello</body></html>

A child's resource completely replaces any ancestor's.

headerOut

A map of response header names to values. Headers from all nodes along the matched path are merged: later (child) entries override earlier (parent) entries for the same header name; all other headers are preserved. 

negotiated:
  headerOut:
    X-Server: MockingHAL      # added to every response from this subtree
  method:
    GET:
      headerOut:
        Content-Type: application/json   # added for GET
      resource: {"ok": true}
    POST:
      code: 201
      headerOut:
        Content-Type: application/json
        Location: /new-resource          # POST adds Location too
MockingHAL does not set Content-Type automatically. Always include it in headerOut when the response body has a specific media type. If absent, the server falls back to application/json.

Accumulation example

For a GET /items/42 request against this config: 

items:
  headerOut:
    X-Api: "1.0"           # ← always present
  code: 200
  path:
    /items:
      method:
        GET:
          headerOut:
            Content-Type: application/json  # ← added for GET
          path:
            "/[0-9]+":
              resource: {id: 42, name: "Widget"}  # ← body

The final response is: 200 OK, headers X-Api: 1.0 and Content-Type: application/json, body {"id":42,"name":"Widget"}.

/combining.resource:
Docs › Config guide › Combining filters

Combining Filters

Any node may carry any number of filter fields simultaneously. All filters at a node are evaluated as AND conditions: every filter present must pass (or, for selectors, fall through) for the match to succeed.

Child chaining

When multiple filters at a node each match, they each contribute one child node. These children are chained in a fixed order: 

method → path → query → headerIn → body

The accumulated response from the current node becomes the starting point. Each child in the chain can then override or extend it. The chain is processed sequentially; the result of one child becomes the input to the next.

Example: method + headerIn at the same node

Put shared response defaults on the node and use a selector to override for specific cases — no repetition needed: 

api_root:
  path:
    /:
      method:
        GET:
          code: 200
          headerOut:
            Content-Type: application/json  # default content type
          resource:
            _links: {self: {href: /}}       # default body
          headerIn:
            Accept:
              "application/hal\\+xml":       # override for XML clients
                headerOut:
                  Content-Type: application/hal+xml
                resource: ""

A GET / with no Accept header returns the JSON body. A GET / with Accept: application/hal+xml returns the XML body (the headerIn child overrides both resource and Content-Type).

Example: method + path at the same node

Hoist shared response elements to the parent and keep routing logic in method/path children: 

items_api:
  code: 200                              # shared default
  headerOut:
    Content-Type: application/json       # shared header
  method:
    GET:
      path:
        "/items/[0-9]+":
          resource: {id: 1, name: "Widget"}
    POST:
      path:
        /items:
          code: 201
          resource: {id: 99, name: "New item"}
    DELETE:
      path:
        "/items/[0-9]+":
          code: 204

The Content-Type: application/json header is inherited by all three method branches. DELETE overrides the code to 204.

Constraints

  • At most one method per root-to-leaf path. Nesting a second method filter inside a method child is technically allowed but only matches if both keys are identical — which is always redundant.
  • Multiple path filters chain. Each one strips its matched prefix before the next is evaluated. This is how multi-segment paths decompose naturally into nested nodes.
  • Multiple headerIn entries at one node are not supported — only the first key is read. To require two distinct headers simultaneously, nest a second headerIn inside the child of the first.
Keep routing logic (method, path) near the top of a node and selectors (query, headerIn, body) near the bottom. This mirrors evaluation order and makes configs easier to read.

/yaml-tips.resource:
Docs › Config guide › YAML tips

YAML Tips

Quoting regex metacharacters

Pattern keys in path, query, body, and the inner maps of headerIn are Java regular expressions. Characters that are special in regex — . * + ? ( ) [ ] { } ^ $ | \ — must be escaped with a backslash when you want them to be literal.

In YAML double-quoted strings, a backslash itself must be doubled. In unquoted or single-quoted strings, a single backslash works: 

# These three are equivalent and all match a literal "hal+json":
headerIn:
  Accept:
    "application/hal\\+json": {}  # double-quoted: \\ → \ in regex
    'application/hal\+json':  {}  # single-quoted: \ preserved
    application/hal\+json:    {}  # unquoted:      \ preserved
The safest habit: always use double-quoted strings for regex patterns and double every backslash. This is unambiguous regardless of context.

Anchors and aliases — DRY configs

YAML anchors (&name) mark a value; aliases (*name) reuse it. This avoids repeating large bodies: 

variants:
  method:
    GET:
      path:
        /report:
          resource: &report_body
            title: Monthly Report
            rows: [1, 2, 3]
        /summary:
          resource: *report_body   # same object, no repetition

Anchors work on any YAML value: strings, maps, lists, or entire subtrees.

Multi-line string styles

StyleSyntaxBest for
Literal block | HTML, XML, pre-formatted text — newlines preserved exactly
Folded block > Long prose — newlines folded to spaces, blank lines become newlines
Folded strip >- Like folded but trailing newline removed — good for description fields
Double-quoted "…" Short strings with escape sequences (\n, \\) 
page:
  resource: |
    <!DOCTYPE html>
    <html>
    <body>Hello</body>
    </html>

description: >-
  This is a long description that wraps across multiple
  lines in the YAML but becomes a single space-separated
  string in the parsed value.

Numeric keys

YAML unquoted values like 200 or true are parsed as numbers or booleans — even when used as map keys. Quote keys that look like numbers to keep them as strings: 

# WRONG: YAML parses 200 as an integer key
path:
  200: {}

# RIGHT: quoted string key
path:
  "200": {}

Flow style for compact inline nodes

Simple leaf nodes can be written on one line using YAML flow style: 

routes:
  method:
    GET:  {code: 200, resource: {ok: true}}
    POST: {code: 201}
    DELETE: {code: 204}

/examples.resource:
Docs › Config guide › Examples

Examples

Each example is a complete configuration you can load with curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/ -H 'Content-Type: application/yaml' --data-binary @file.yaml or send as the body of PATCH / to extend the current config.

1 — Simple CRUD collection

users:
  path:
    /users:
      method:
        GET:
          code: 200
          headerOut:
            Content-Type: application/json
          resource:
            - {id: 1, name: Alice}
            - {id: 2, name: Bob}
        POST:
          code: 201
          headerOut:
            Content-Type: application/json
            Location: /users/3
          resource: {id: 3, name: "New user"}

user_by_id:
  path:
    "/users/[0-9]+":
      method:
        GET:
          code: 200
          headerOut:
            Content-Type: application/json
          resource: {id: 1, name: Alice}
        PUT:
          code: 200
          headerOut:
            Content-Type: application/json
          resource: {id: 1, name: "Updated"}
        DELETE:
          code: 204

2 — Content negotiation

negotiated_root:
  path:
    /:
      method:
        GET:
          code: 200
          headerOut:
            Content-Type: application/json      # default
          resource:
            message: Hello
            format: json
          headerIn:
            Accept:
              "text/html":
                headerOut:
                  Content-Type: text/html
                resource: |
                  <html><body><h1>Hello</h1></body></html>
              "text/plain":
                headerOut:
                  Content-Type: text/plain
                resource: "Hello (plain text)"

3 — Query-driven variants

search_api:
  path:
    /search:
      method:
        GET:
          code: 200
          headerOut:
            Content-Type: application/json
          resource: {results: [], total: 0}    # no-query default
          query:
            "":                                # empty query string
              resource: {error: "q parameter required"}
              code: 400
            "q=error":
              code: 500
              resource: {error: "simulated server error"}
            "q=":
              resource: {results: [], total: 0}
            "q=.+":                            # any non-empty q
              resource:
                results: [{id: 1, title: "Result one"}]
                total: 1

4 — Error simulation

flaky_endpoint:
  path:
    /flaky:
      method:
        GET:
          code: 200
          headerOut:
            Content-Type: application/json
          resource: {status: ok}
          headerIn:
            X-Force-Error:
              "404": {code: 404, resource: {error: "not found"}}
              "500": {code: 500, resource: {error: "server error"}}
              "503": {code: 503, resource: {error: "unavailable"}}

Send X-Force-Error: 503 to simulate a service-unavailable response from any client without changing the server config.

5 — Body-conditional response

command_handler:
  path:
    /commands:
      method:
        POST:
          code: 202
          headerOut:
            Content-Type: application/json
          resource: {status: accepted}
          body:
            '"action"\s*:\s*"shutdown"':
              code: 200
              resource: {status: "shutting down"}
            '"action"\s*:\s*"restart"':
              code: 200
              resource: {status: "restarting"}
            '"action"\s*:\s*"unknown"':
              code: 400
              resource: {error: "unknown action"}

6 — Shared defaults with per-method overrides

api:
  code: 200
  headerOut:
    Content-Type: application/json
    X-Api-Version: "2"
  method:
    GET:
      path:
        /items:
          resource: [{id: 1}, {id: 2}]
        "/items/[0-9]+":
          resource: {id: 1, name: Widget}
    POST:
      path:
        /items:
          code: 201
          headerOut:
            Location: /items/99
          resource: {id: 99}
    DELETE:
      path:
        "/items/[0-9]+":
          code: 204

code: 200, Content-Type: application/json, and X-Api-Version: 2 are inherited by all branches. POST /items overrides the code to 201 and adds a Location header. DELETE overrides to 204 with no body.
